attachment.content:
- animate: Review a feature and enhance it with purposeful animations, micro-interactions, and motion effects that improve usability and delight. Use when the user mentions adding animation, transitions, micro-interactions, motion design, hover effects, or making the UI feel more alive.
- quieter: Tones down visually aggressive or overstimulating designs, reducing intensity while preserving quality. Use when the user mentions too bold, too loud, overwhelming, aggressive, garish, or wants a calmer, more refined aesthetic.
- userinterface-wiki: UI/UX best practices for web interfaces. Use when reviewing animations, CSS, audio, typography, UX patterns, prefetching, or icon implementations. Covers 11 categories from animation principles to typography. Outputs file:line findings.
- optimize: Diagnoses and fixes UI performance across loading speed, rendering, animations, images, and bundle size. Use when the user mentions slow, laggy, janky, performance, bundle size, load time, or wants a faster, smoother experience.
- adapt: Adapt designs to work across different screen sizes, devices, contexts, or platforms. Implements breakpoints, fluid layouts, and touch targets. Use when the user mentions responsive design, mobile layouts, breakpoints, viewport adaptation, or cross-device compatibility.
- code-edit: Use Fast Apply (edit_file) for precise edits without reading full files.
- mastra: Comprehensive Mastra framework guide. Teaches how to find current documentation, verify API signatures, and build agents and workflows. Covers documentation lookup strategies (embedded docs, remote docs), core concepts (agents vs workflows, tools, memory, RAG), TypeScript requirements, and common patterns. Use this skill for all Mastra development to ensure you're using current APIs from the installed version or latest documentation.
- clarify: Improve unclear UX copy, error messages, microcopy, labels, and instructions to make interfaces easier to understand. Use when the user mentions confusing text, unclear labels, bad error messages, hard-to-follow instructions, or wanting better UX writing.
- distill: Strip designs to their essence by removing unnecessary complexity. Great design is simple, powerful, and clean. Use when the user asks to simplify, declutter, reduce noise, remove elements, or make a UI cleaner and more focused.
- delight: Add moments of joy, personality, and unexpected touches that make interfaces memorable and enjoyable to use. Elevates functional to delightful. Use when the user asks to add polish, personality, animations, micro-interactions, delight, or make an interface feel fun or memorable.
- teach-impeccable: One-time setup that gathers design context for your project and saves it to your AI config file. Run once to establish persistent design guidelines.
- onboard: Designs and improves onboarding flows, empty states, and first-run experiences to help users reach value quickly. Use when the user mentions onboarding, first-time users, empty states, activation, getting started, or new user flows.
- explore: Use this skill when the user asks you something that requires searching a code base
- normalize: Audits and realigns UI to match design system standards, spacing, tokens, and patterns. Use when the user mentions consistency, design drift, mismatched styles, tokens, or wants to bring a feature back in line with the system.
- audit: Run technical quality checks across accessibility, performance, theming, responsive design, and anti-patterns. Generates a scored report with P0-P3 severity ratings and actionable plan. Use when the user wants an accessibility check, performance audit, or technical quality review.
- harden: Improve interface resilience through better error handling, i18n support, text overflow handling, and edge case management. Makes interfaces robust and production-ready. Use when the user asks to harden, make production-ready, handle edge cases, add error states, or fix overflow and i18n issues.
- polish: Performs a final quality pass fixing alignment, spacing, consistency, and micro-detail issues before shipping. Use when the user mentions polish, finishing touches, pre-launch review, something looks off, or wants to go from good to great.
- feature-research: Research existing architecture before implementing a complex feature.
- extract: Extract and consolidate reusable components, design tokens, and patterns into your design system. Identifies opportunities for systematic reuse and enriches your component library. Use when the user asks to create components, refactor repeated UI patterns, build a design system, or extract tokens.
- bolder: Amplify safe or boring designs to make them more visually interesting and stimulating. Increases impact while maintaining usability. Use when the user says the design looks bland, generic, too safe, lacks personality, or wants more visual impact and character.
- frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics. Use when the user asks to build web components, pages, artifacts, posters, or applications, or when any design skill requires project context.
- arrange: Improve layout, spacing, and visual rhythm. Fixes monotonous grids, inconsistent spacing, and weak visual hierarchy. Use when the user mentions layout feeling off, spacing issues, visual hierarchy, crowded UI, alignment problems, or wanting better composition.
- typeset: Improves typography by fixing font choices, hierarchy, sizing, weight, and readability so text feels intentional. Use when the user mentions fonts, type, readability, text hierarchy, sizing looks off, or wants more polished, intentional typography.
- tanstack-start-best-practices: TanStack Start best practices for full-stack React applications. Server functions, middleware, SSR, authentication, and deployment patterns. Activate when building full-stack apps with TanStack Start.
- critique: Evaluate design from a UX perspective, assessing visual hierarchy, information architecture, emotional resonance, cognitive load, and overall quality with quantitative scoring, persona-based testing, and actionable feedback. Use when the user asks to review, critique, evaluate, or give feedback on a design or component.
- presentation-design: Design and evaluate presentations that communicate effectively. Use when designing a presentation, creating slides, getting presentation feedback, structuring a talk, or reviewing slides. Keywords: presentation, slides, talk, PowerPoint, Keynote, reveal.js.
- colorize: Add strategic color to features that are too monochromatic or lack visual interest, making interfaces more engaging and expressive. Use when the user mentions the design looking gray, dull, lacking warmth, needing more color, or wanting a more vibrant or expressive palette.
- overdrive: Pushes interfaces past conventional limits with technically ambitious implementations — shaders, spring physics, scroll-driven reveals, 60fps animations. Use when the user wants to wow, impress, go all-out, or make something that feels extraordinary.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll check how the show page displays expenses and whether it indicates venue-absorbed expenses.

Expenses are reported, not deducted from artist payout.` 628 : "Flat guarantee; missing guarantee amount."; 629 case "percentage_of_gross": 630 return deal.percentage != null 631 ? `${formatPct(deal.percentage)} of gross box office.` 632 : "% of gross deal; missing percentage."; 633 case "percentage_of_net": 634 return deal.percentage != null 635 ? `${formatPct(deal.percentage)} of net after expenses${deal.expenseCap != null ? `, capped at ${formatMoney(deal.expenseCap)}` : ""}.` 636 : "% of net deal; missing percentage."; 637 case "vs": { 638 const guarantee = 639 deal.guaranteeAmount != null ? formatMoney(deal.guaranteeAmount) : "missing guarantee"; 640 const pct = 641 deal.percentage != null ? formatPct(deal.percentage) : "missing percentage"; 642 const basis = 643 deal.percentageBasis === "gross" 644 ? "gross" 645 : `net after expenses${deal.expenseCap != null ? ` capped at ${formatMoney(deal.expenseCap)}` : ""}`; 646 return `max(${guarantee} guarantee, ${pct} of ${basis}).`; 647 } 648 case "door": 649 return "Door deal. Ticketing data does not split door receipts from presales yet."; 650 } 651 } 652 653 function recoupAmountLabel(clause: RecoupClause) { 654 if (clause.amountModel === "fixed") { 655 return clause.fixedAmount != null ? formatMoney(clause.fixedAmount) : "fixed amount missing"; 656 } 657 if (clause.amountModel === "actual_up_to") { 658 return clause.capAmount != null 659 ? `actuals up to ${formatMoney(clause.capAmount)}` 660 : "actuals up to missing cap"; 661 } 662 return "actuals uncapped"; 663 } 664 665 function formatTier( 666 tier: Extract["tiers"][number], 667 allTiers: Extract["tiers"], 668 ) { 669 // TODO: drop this heuristic once tier_ratchet carries an explicit `basis` discriminator. 670 // See lib/dealMath.ts → inferRatchetBasis for the full story. 671 const isCapacityFraction = allTiers.some( 672 (t) => 673 (t.from > 0 && t.from < 1) || 674 (t.to != null && t.to > 0 && t.to < 1), 675 ); 676 if (isCapacityFraction) { 677 const from = formatPct(tier.from); 678 const to = tier.to == null ? "up" : formatPct(tier.to); 679 return `${from}-${to} sold @ ${formatPct(tier.percentage)}`; 680 } 681 const from = formatMoney(tier.from); 682 const to = tier.to == null ? "up" : formatMoney(tier.to); 683 return `${from}-${to} @ ${formatPct(tier.percentage)}`; 684 } 685 686 function formatPct(value: number) { 687 return `${(value * 100).toFixed(value * 100 >= 10 ? 0 : 1)}%`; 688 } 689

lastPrompt: does epenses card in /show page have any indicator of expenses having been abosrbed by venue if in db

content.text:
Yes — the Expenses card has two indicators for venue-absorbed expenses:

1. **Header badge** at [app/shows/[id]/page.tsx:542-546](app/shows/[id]/page.tsx#L542-L546) — an amber `PlainBadge` showing the total absorbed amount (e.g. "$450 absorbed"), only rendered when `absorbedTotal > 0`.

2. **Per-row badge** at [app/shows/[id]/page.tsx:567-569](app/shows/[id]/page.tsx#L567-L569) — an amber "absorbed" `PlainBadge` next to the category for each individual expense where `e.absorbedByVenue` is true.

Additionally, absorbed expenses are excluded from the displayed "Total (passed through)" via the filter at [app/shows/[id]/page.tsx:85-87](app/shows/[id]/page.tsx#L85-L87), so they don't inflate the pass-through total.

input.description: Find max( formula references on settle page

content.text: Found it — the string is built in [lib/dealMath.ts:407](lib/dealMath.ts#L407) as `finalFormula`. Let me find where the settle page renders it.
Found it. I'll remove the formula from the worksheet's `CardDescription`.
